fa: The lightest and most abundant element in the universe, making up 75% of all normal matter by mass and fuelling stars through nuclear fusion
First detected in the Sun's spectrum during a solar eclipse before ever being found on Earth; its name comes from Helios, the Greek god of the Sun
The lightest metal and lightest solid element — so light it floats on water, though it reacts violently with it; powers the batteries in electric vehicles
Extremely light, stiff, and transparent to X-rays; used in spacecraft windows and nuclear reactor components, but highly toxic when inhaled as dust
Pure boron is extremely hard and used in high-strength materials; borosilicate glass (Pyrex) owes its remarkable thermal resistance to boron
The basis of all known life — forms more compounds than any other element; its allotropes range from soft graphite to diamond, the hardest naturally occurring mineral
Makes up 78% of Earth's atmosphere and is essential for all living organisms as a component of amino acids, proteins, and DNA; liquid nitrogen boils at -196 C
Constitutes 21% of Earth's atmosphere and is essential for respiration; liquid oxygen is bright blue and so reactive it can cause organic materials to combust spontaneously
The most electronegative and reactive element — so reactive it can ignite glass and water; used in non-stick Teflon coatings and added to toothpaste to strengthen tooth enamel
Famous for its brilliant orange-red glow in electric discharge tubes; despite being the fifth most abundant element in the universe, Earth's atmosphere contains only trace amounts
A soft silvery metal that reacts violently with water; combined with chlorine it forms table salt (NaCl), essential to life; its symbol Na comes from the Latin 'Natrium'
Burns with an intense white flame used in flares and fireworks; the central atom of chlorophyll, it is essential to all plant photosynthesis on Earth
The most abundant metal in Earth's crust; once more valuable than gold, it is now used in aircraft, packaging, and construction — 100% recyclable without loss of quality
The second most abundant element in Earth's crust and the semiconductor that underpins the entire modern computing industry — 'Silicon Valley' is named after it
Essential for DNA, RNA, and ATP — life's energy currency; white phosphorus glows in the dark and ignites spontaneously in air; the first element discovered by a scientist
Known as 'brimstone' in the Bible and found abundantly near volcanoes; crucial for fertilizers, rubber vulcanization, and was an ingredient in gunpowder for centuries
A toxic yellow-green gas used to purify drinking water worldwide and deployed as the first chemical weapon in WWI; combined with sodium it forms common table salt
The third most abundant gas in Earth's atmosphere at 0.93%; used to fill incandescent light bulbs, as a welding protective atmosphere, and to insulate double-glazed windows
Essential for life — regulates nerve impulses and muscle contractions in every living organism; its symbol K comes from the Latin 'Kalium'
The fifth most abundant element in Earth's crust and the most abundant mineral in the human body; essential for bones, teeth, and the shells of marine organisms
Predicted by Mendeleev before its discovery; when alloyed with aluminum it creates an exceptionally strong, light material used in aerospace structures and sports equipment
As strong as steel but 45% lighter, with exceptional corrosion resistance and biocompatibility; the metal of choice for surgical implants, dental prosthetics, and aerospace engineering
Named after Vanadis, the Norse goddess of beauty; strengthens steel in tools and car axles, and is found in the blood of some sea squirts — uniquely among metals
Gives rubies their red color and emeralds their green; the 'chrome' in stainless steel and chrome plating provides mirror-like shine and exceptional corrosion resistance
Essential for steel production — most steel contains about 1.2% manganese; also an important trace mineral for the human body, involved in bone formation and metabolism
The most used metal in human history; forms Earth's solid inner core and its liquid outer core, generating the magnetic field that protects all life from harmful solar radiation
Gives a vivid blue color to glass, ceramics, and medieval stained glass; essential for vitamin B12 production and a critical material in lithium-ion batteries for EVs
Hard, lustrous, and corrosion-resistant; one of only four magnetic elements at room temperature; its name comes from 'Kupfernickel' (copper demon) as miners mistook it for copper ore
One of the first metals used by humans; the best electrical conductor after silver, it carries power through virtually every electrical cable and device on Earth
Essential for immune function and wound healing; used to galvanize steel against rust and is the active UV-blocking ingredient in mineral sunscreen
Melts in the palm of your hand at just 29.8 C; predicted by Mendeleev before its discovery, its detection was a major confirmation of the periodic table
Predicted as 'eka-silicon' by Mendeleev before its discovery, perfectly validating his periodic table; now used in fiber optics and infrared camera lenses
One of history's most notorious poisons, called 'inheritance powder' in medieval times; paradoxically used as medicine for centuries; now used in semiconductors and wood preservatives
Named after the Moon goddess Selene; its electrical conductivity increases with light, making it the key element in the first photocopiers (xerography) and early solar cells
One of only two elements liquid at room temperature; its name means 'stench' in Greek; used in flame retardants and was once added to leaded gasoline
Used in high-performance lighting and laser technology; from 1960 to 1983 the international meter was officially defined as 1,650,763.73 wavelengths of orange krypton-86 light
So soft it can be cut with a knife and ignites spontaneously in air; used in atomic clocks and produces the purple-red color in fireworks
Produces the brilliant crimson-red color in fireworks and road flares; the radioactive isotope Sr-90 was a major Cold War health concern as it accumulates in bones
Named after Ytterby, a Swedish village that also gave its name to erbium, terbium, and ytterbium; used in LED phosphors and the YAG lasers used in surgery
Cubic zirconia (ZrO2) is a popular diamond simulant in jewelry; the pure metal is used as cladding for nuclear fuel rods due to its very low neutron absorption
A superconductor below -264 C; used in the superconducting magnets of MRI scanners and CERN's Large Hadron Collider
A component of enzymes that allow soil bacteria to fix atmospheric nitrogen; also used in high-speed steel cutting tools because it retains hardness at extreme temperatures
The first element ever produced artificially — its name means 'artificial' in Greek; the only element below bismuth with no stable isotopes; widely used in nuclear medicine imaging
One of the rarest metals, named after Russia; used as a hardening agent in platinum and palladium alloys and as a catalyst; increasingly important in solar cells
One of the rarest and most expensive metals; most rhodium is used in the three-way catalytic converters in car exhaust systems, reducing toxic emissions by up to 99%
Can absorb up to 900 times its own volume of hydrogen gas; named after the asteroid Pallas; a key component in automotive catalytic converters and electronics
The most electrically and thermally conductive element; used in jewelry, electronics, photography, and as a natural antimicrobial agent in medical devices and clothing
Produces bright yellow, orange, and red pigments used by artists; highly toxic — the Itai-itai disease in Japan was caused by cadmium contamination of rice paddies
Emits a high-pitched 'cry' when bent; indium tin oxide (ITO) is the transparent conductive coating found on the touchscreen of every smartphone and tablet
Combined with copper to create bronze, tin ushered in the Bronze Age ~3300 BC; produces a squealing 'tin cry' when bent; used in food cans, solder, and protective coatings
Used as kohl eye makeup in ancient Egypt and as medicine for thousands of years; today used as a flame retardant in plastics and electronics and in lead-acid car batteries
One of the rarest stable elements in Earth's crust; gives garlic breath to anyone who touches it; used in CdTe solar panels, the most cost-effective photovoltaic technology
Essential for thyroid hormone production; at room temperature it sublimes directly to a beautiful violet vapor; its antiseptic properties have been invaluable in medicine for over 200 years
Used in high-intensity cinema projector lamps and satellite ion drives; also used as a surgical anesthetic; the name means 'stranger' in Greek
The basis of the most accurate timekeepers ever built — atomic clocks; the international second is defined by 9,192,631,770 oscillations of cesium-133 atoms
Barium sulfate is opaque to X-rays, making it the key ingredient in 'barium meals' used to visualize the digestive tract in medical imaging
The first of the lanthanide series, giving the group its name; used in high-quality camera lenses to reduce chromatic aberration and in hydrogen storage for fuel cells
The most abundant rare earth element; used in catalytic converters to reduce car emissions, in glass polishing compounds, and in the sparks of cigarette lighter flints
Its name means 'green twin' in Greek; used in powerful rare-earth magnets, aircraft engine alloys, and gives glass a distinctive yellow-green color used by glassblowers
Creates the strongest permanent magnets known to science; the neodymium magnets in electric motors, hard drives, wind turbines, and headphones underpin modern technology
The only naturally radioactive lanthanide with no stable isotopes; named after Prometheus who stole fire from the gods; once used in luminous watch dials and pacemaker batteries
Used in samarium-cobalt magnets that retain magnetism at very high temperatures; found in jet engines, electric motors in hostile environments, and precision-guided targeting systems
Produces the red and blue colors in TV screens and energy-efficient fluorescent lamps; added to euro banknotes as an invisible UV fluorescent security feature
Injected as a contrast agent to enhance MRI scans; has the highest neutron absorption of any element, making it invaluable in nuclear reactor control rods
Used in green phosphors in fluorescent lamps and TV tubes; its alloy Terfenol-D is used in sonar transducers and underwater acoustic systems
Added to neodymium magnets to allow them to work at higher temperatures; essential for the electric motors in hybrid and electric vehicles operating under the hood
Named after Stockholm (Holmia); has the strongest magnetic moment of any element; used to create the most powerful steady-state magnetic fields for physics research
Gives a pink color to glass; erbium-doped fiber optic cables are the backbone of long-distance internet, amplifying light signals without converting them to electricity
Named after Thule, the ancient name for Scandinavia; one of the rarest rare earth elements; used in portable X-ray devices that require no electrical power source
Named after Ytterby, Sweden — one of four elements from the same village; ytterbium atomic clocks lose less than 1 second in 10 billion years
Named after Lutetia, the Roman name for Paris; the densest rare earth element, used as a catalyst in petroleum refining and in PET scan detectors for cancer diagnosis
Almost always found alongside zirconium; used in Intel microprocessors since 2007 to prevent current leakage, enabling the continued miniaturization of transistors
Virtually immune to chemical attack; the capacitors in every smartphone and computer contain tantalum; much of the world's supply comes from conflict zones in the DRC
The highest melting point of any metal at 3,422 C; used in light bulb filaments, X-ray tubes, and rocket nozzles; its symbol W comes from the German name 'Wolfram'
One of the rarest elements in Earth's crust with the third highest melting point; used in superalloys for jet engine turbine blades that must withstand extreme temperatures
The densest naturally occurring element at 22.59 g/cm3 — an osmium cube the size of a basketball would weigh over 400 kg; its name means 'smell' due to its acrid, toxic oxide
The most corrosion-resistant metal known; a global iridium-rich layer in rock marks the asteroid impact 66 million years ago that killed the non-avian dinosaurs
One of the rarest and most valuable metals; used in catalytic converters, cancer chemotherapy drugs (cisplatin), and jewelry; once served as the international prototype kilogram
One of the least reactive elements — it doesn't tarnish or corrode; a universal symbol of value for millennia; most gold on Earth arrived via meteorite impacts billions of years ago
The only metal liquid at standard room temperature; its symbol Hg comes from 'Hydrargyrum' (liquid silver); once used in thermometers and barometers, now largely replaced due to toxicity
Highly toxic and causes hair loss, making it a favoured poison in murder cases; used in specialized optical glass and was once widely used as a rat poison
One of the earliest metals used by humans; Romans used it in plumbing and wine vessels; its neurotoxicity, now well understood, caused widespread health problems throughout history
Forms spectacular iridescent staircase crystals; used in Pepto-Bismol; the heaviest element with a stable isotope, though Bi-209 has a half-life 10 billion times the age of the universe
Discovered by Marie Curie and named after her homeland Poland; highly radioactive; was used to poison Russian spy Alexander Litvinenko in London in 2006
The rarest naturally occurring element — the entire Earth contains less than 30 grams at any given time; so radioactive it would vaporize itself and any container holding a visible sample
A naturally occurring radioactive gas produced by uranium and radium decay in rocks and soil; seeps into buildings and is the second leading cause of lung cancer after smoking
Discovered by French physicist Marguerite Perey; so rare that at any moment there are fewer than 30 grams on the entire Earth; the most unstable of the naturally occurring elements
Discovered by Marie Curie in 1898; glows faint blue in the dark due to radioactivity; once used in luminous watch dials until the deadly fate of the 'Radium Girls' factory workers became known
The first of the actinide series; so radioactive it glows blue in the dark due to ionization of surrounding air; used in experimental targeted cancer radiation therapy
Named after Thor, the Norse god; three times more abundant than uranium; a potential nuclear fuel for safer reactors that produce significantly less long-lived radioactive waste
One of the rarest and most expensive natural elements; its name means 'before actinium' as it decays into actinium; only about 125 grams exist worldwide for scientific research
The heaviest naturally occurring element; its nuclear fission powers reactors generating about 10% of global electricity; also the fuel for nuclear weapons
The first transuranic element ever synthesized; named after Neptune, the planet beyond Uranus, just as neptunium is beyond uranium in the periodic table
Produced in nuclear reactors; Pu-239 is a primary material in nuclear weapons; also used as fuel in NASA space probe RTGs — powering Voyager 1 and 2 beyond our solar system
Found in smoke detectors worldwide — a tiny amount of Am-241 ionizes air to detect smoke particles; discovered during the Manhattan Project but not announced until after WWII ended
Named after Marie and Pierre Curie; glows red-hot due to intense radioactivity; used as a power source in NASA's Mars Science Laboratory alpha particle X-ray spectrometer on Curiosity
Named after Berkeley, California; only a few micrograms have ever been produced; it was used as the target material in the experiment that first synthesized element 117 (tennessine)
One of the strongest neutron sources known; used to start nuclear reactors and in portable neutron moisture gauges to scan oil wells and measure water content in soil
Discovered in the fallout of the first hydrogen bomb test in 1952, classified for years; named after Albert Einstein; only about 3 micrograms have ever been produced in total
Named after Enrico Fermi; also discovered in H-bomb fallout in 1952; the heaviest element that can be produced by neutron bombardment of lighter elements
Named after Dmitri Mendeleev, creator of the periodic table; only one atom at a time has ever been detected; required a cyclotron to bombard einsteinium to produce it
Named after Alfred Nobel; its discovery was hotly contested between Swedish and Soviet scientists for years; only a few atoms are produced at a time and they decay within seconds
Named after Ernest Lawrence, inventor of the cyclotron; the last of the actinides; like all transuranic elements it is made in particle accelerators and decays within hours
Named after Ernest Rutherford; the first transactinide element — the first to go beyond the actinide series; only a few atoms are produced at a time in particle accelerators
Named after Dubna, Russia; its discovery was contested between Soviet and American scientists during the Cold War; behaves chemically like niobium and tantalum
Named after Glenn T. Seaborg — the only element named after a living person at the time; he had already won the Nobel Prize for discovering ten transuranic elements
Named after Niels Bohr; its most stable isotope has a half-life of 61 seconds; produced and detected one atom at a time — no practical uses exist for this superheavy element
Named after the German state of Hesse; experiments suggest it behaves like osmium, its lighter group 8 congener; one of the heaviest elements with a confirmed decay chain
Named after Lise Meitner, who co-discovered nuclear fission; fewer than 10 atoms have ever been detected; named in her honor after she was controversially excluded from the Nobel Prize
Named after Darmstadt, Germany; only a few atoms have ever been created; with a half-life of just 11 seconds for its most stable isotope, its chemical properties remain nearly unknown
Named after Wilhelm Rontgen, discoverer of X-rays; produced one atom at a time; only about 30 atoms have ever been detected and they decay within microseconds
Named after astronomer Nicolaus Copernicus; relativistic effects are so strong that it may behave as a noble gas rather than a metal — predicted to be gaseous at room temperature
Named after Japan (Nihon); the first element discovered in Asia, by a RIKEN team in 2004; officially named in 2016, making Japan the first Asian country to name a new element
Named after the Flerov Laboratory in Dubna; relativistic effects may make it behave like a noble gas; physicists think it may be near the predicted 'island of stability' of superheavy elements
Named after Moscow Oblast, Russia; confirmed in 2015 after more than a decade of experiments; only a few atoms have ever been created, each decaying within milliseconds
Named after the Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory in California; produced in joint Russian-American research; decays within milliseconds through a chain of new superheavy elements
Named after Tennessee, home to Oak Ridge, Vanderbilt, and the University of Tennessee; the second-heaviest known element; created by bombarding californium-249 with calcium-48 ions
The heaviest element ever synthesized and the last in the current periodic table; named after nuclear physicist Yuri Oganessian; only 5 atoms have ever been detected
